answer: Dinka nouns exhibit complex morphological changes for singular and plural forms, primarily involving systematic shifts in vowel length, tone, and vowel quality. There are two main opposing tendencies: one involves vowel lengthening and tone lowering (high to low), and the other involves vowel shortening and tone raising (falling/low to high). Consonant changes are less frequent. Dinka verb conjugations also show vowel length and quality changes, primarily lengthening from the root. Tone changes in verbs are present but less uniform than in nouns, and consonant changes are specific to certain verbs. Verb patterns appear more verb-specific than the broader noun patterns.
